title: Post-Breach BIN Range Exploitation with Incremental Testing Phase
narrative: A newly compromised BIN range (typically first 8 digits) exhibits coordinated testing followed by rapid monetization. Fraudsters acquire a batch of cards from a single breach source and systematically test them before executing larger purchases. The pattern shows 15-40 cards from the same IIN (e.g., 442673XX) appearing within a 6-12 hour window, initially making small authorization attempts ($1-$9.99) at low-risk merchants (MCC 5999, 5818), then escalating to high-value CNP transactions ($200-$800) at electronics or gaming merchants within 90 minutes of successful test. Cards share no legitimate linkageâ€”different addr1 values, mixed P_emaildomain patterns (often temporary email services), and dist1 indicating impossible geographic separation between billing/shipping. The BIN shows card1_txn_count_1day jumping from baseline 2-5 to 150+ transactions, with 70%+ occurring during is_night=1 hours. TransactionAmt distribution shifts dramatically from normal patterns for that issuer.
indicators: 15-40 unique card numbers from same 8-digit BIN prefix appearing within 6-12 hour window
Two-stage pattern: initial $1-$9.99 test transactions followed by $200-$800+ purchases within 90 minutes per card
card1_txn_count_1day for the BIN range exceeds 150 transactions versus typical baseline of 2-5 per day
70%+ of transactions occur during is_night=1 with txn_hour concentrated in 1-6 AM range
addr1 diversity score >0.85 indicating no geographic clustering despite same BIN source
P_emaildomain shows 60%+ temporary/disposable email services (guerrillamail, tempmail, 10minutemail)
dist1 values average >500km between billing/shipping addresses with multiple international destinations
summary: A batch of 15-40 cards sharing the same 8-digit BIN suddenly appears within hours, first making small test purchases under $10, then escalating to high-value transactions ($200-$800) within 90 minutes if the test succeeds. The cards show no legitimate connection—different addresses, disposable emails, impossible geographic separations—and the BIN's daily transaction volume spikes from 2-5 to over 150 transactions, mostly at night.
reasoning: Fraudsters have acquired a fresh batch of stolen cards from the same data breach and are systematically validating which ones still work before draining their limits. The coordinated two-stage approach—test then exploit—combined with temporary emails and mismatched geography reveals organized criminals monetizing compromised payment data rather than legitimate cardholders making independent purchases.
indicator_explanations: Dozens of cards from the same bank batch hit the system in rapid succession
Each card follows the test-then-exploit pattern: small probe followed by major charge
The entire bank range generates wildly more transactions than any legitimate cardholder baseline
Attack executes during graveyard shift when fraud monitoring coverage is weakest
Cards from same bank are used across completely scattered geographic locations
Attacker uses throwaway email addresses to register the stolen card numbers
Shipping and billing locations span different countries with massive geographic separation
